work.intro_1: My best work happens where constraints are hard, behaviour is messy, and the brief sounds like: “Nothing works. Do whatever you think is right — but it has to work.”
work.intro_2: My projects are driven by pattern‑based reasoning, predictive cognition, and systems thinking. What I describe as intuition is, in practice, a form of rapid cognitive modelling — I recognise the underlying structure of a problem and generate the architecture of a solution before any formal analysis begins. From there, I move through an iterative SPINE cycle: system grounding → cognitive modelling → behavioural friction mapping → hypothesis architecture → mixed‑methods integration → systemic implementation.
work.list_how_1_1: R&D first — I prototype, test, break, and iterate fast.
work.list_how_1_2: Framework‑driven — CIR, FEDRI and SPINE guide decisions when intuition is not enough.
work.list_how_1_3: Constraint‑native — I treat budget, legacy, and politics as design inputs, not obstacles.
work.list_how_2_1: Systems — I see projects as living systems, not static features.
work.list_how_2_2: Cognitive — I model how people actually behave, not how they “should”.
work.list_how_2_3: Emergent — I design for resilience, not for perfect control.
work.list_how_2_4: Predictive — I anticipate behavioural outcomes before formal analysis.
work.h_how_3: Where I add the most value
work.list_how_3_1: Early‑stage R&D, prototyping, and concept architecture.
work.list_how_3_2: Behavioural and cognitive systems in constrained environments.
work.list_how_3_3: Translating messy reality into stable frameworks and interventions.
work.list_how_3_4: Rapid formalisation of intuitive insights into scalable architectures.
work.imi_link: What happens when an R&D engineer realises a critical module is missing from her architecture and ends up... generating it from within an incomplete system →
work.dishes_link: What Happens When an R&D Engineer Avoids Doing the Dishes, Degrades the Kitchen System, and Ends Up… Buying More Dishes →
work.list_how_4_4: Cognitive R&D in the kitchen (narrative + agent simulations)
work.dont_1: I don’t follow rigid checklists, predefined workflows, or “just build what we said” instructions. If a project requires blind execution without understanding the system, I’m the wrong person.
work.dont_2: I don’t simplify problems just to make them look neat. If the real issue is messy, emotional, behavioural, or systemic — that’s where I work best. If the expectation is “make it look simple even if it’s not”, I decline.
work.h2_dont_3: Designing without access to real behaviour
work.dont_3: I don’t design in a vacuum. If I can’t observe behaviour, test hypotheses, or validate assumptions, the work won’t be meaningful — and I won’t take it.
work.dont_4: I don’t detach from the emotional and cognitive reality of users. If a team wants “clean data without human messiness”, I’m not the right match.
work.dont_5: I don’t design fantasy solutions that ignore time, budget, politics, legacy systems — or the law. If a team wants “innovation without reality”, including removing legal safeguards, bypassing regulatory requirements, or shipping features that enable harmful or illegal user actions, I step back. I design systems that prevent unsafe behaviour, not ones that allow it.
work.dont_6: I don’t work in cultures where questioning assumptions is treated as a threat, where evidence is ignored because it’s inconvenient, or where accuracy matters less than obedience. If a team expects me to suppress analysis to fit a narrative, I step back.
work.h2_dont_7: Work that requires masking or shrinking
work.dont_7: I don’t tone down my intensity, curiosity, or cognitive speed to fit into environments that fear unconventional thinking. If a team needs someone “less much”, I’m not that person.
work.h2_dont_8: Work that requires ignoring my predictive intuition
work.dont_8: I don’t suppress early pattern recognition just to follow a slow, linear process. If a team needs someone who “waits for permission to think”, I’m not that person.
work.dont_9: I don’t produce personas, journey maps, or glossy artefacts created solely for presentation theatre. If a deliverable doesn’t change behaviour, inform decisions, or improve the system, I don’t make it.

frameworks.into_description: These are the core cognitive and system frameworks I use to structure research, design, and implementation. They are all battle‑tested in real environments (excluding IMI which is still in development phase).
frameworks.into_description_pic: High‑level architecture of my cognitive frameworks — the interaction model between SPINE, CIR, FEDRI, MAKO, MAZE and IMI.
frameworks.spine_description: End‑to‑end research and intervention framework for environments where behaviour is shaped by emotional load, cognitive constraints, structural limitations, or neurodivergent patterns.
frameworks.cir_description: A five‑level model of cognitive stability that explains how emotional load and stress alter information processing, interpretation, and behavioural control.
frameworks.fedri_description: A multi‑resolution cognitive engine for agent modelling, behavioural simulation, and emergent narrative systems, used to generate psychologically coherent agents and predict behaviour under pressure, load, and complex constraints.
frameworks.mako_description: A behavioural system for learning environments where users actively avoid the core activity (e.g. maths). MAKO builds a meta‑layer over content to reframe effort, risk, and reward
frameworks.maze_description: A cognitive architecture for agent‑like tasks, emotional‑load propagation, and fractal planning. First implemented in SelfScape, a neurodivergent‑friendly planning system.
frameworks.imi_link: What happens when an R&D engineer realises a critical module is missing from her architecture and ends up... generating it from within an incomplete system →
frameworks.h_ip_intro_1: CIR, FEDRI, MAZE, MAKO, SPINE and IMI are original cognitive frameworks that I developed independently prior to any employment. They form the conceptual backbone of my work in cognitive modelling, behavioural design, and system‑level analysis.
frameworks.h_ip_intro_2: When I apply these frameworks within an organisation, all implementations created as part of my role — such as code, documentation, prototypes, or internal tools — become the property of that organisation. The frameworks themselves, including their structure, logic, theoretical extensions, and future development, remain my intellectual work and authorship.
frameworks.h_ip_intro_3: This distinction reflects standard practice in R&D and cognitive systems:
frameworks.list_ip_1: theory and methodology belong to the researcher,
frameworks.list_ip_2: applied artefacts belong to the organisation.
frameworks.h_ip_intro_4: I integrate my frameworks through clear interfaces and shared conceptual layers, ensuring compatibility with existing tools, workflows, and team practices while maintaining transparent authorship of the underlying models.
frameworks.h_ip_intro_5: These frameworks are open for conceptual use, but authorship remains mine. Extensions must reference the original models.

sudoku.title: Sudoku Board Generator (TDD, typing, architecture)
sudoku.intro1: This case study presents my real TDD workflow while building a Sudoku board generator.
sudoku.intro2: The entire process was recorded as an 8‑hour timelapse. Below I highlight the most important fragments — the ones that best show how I think, design, and iterate.
sudoku.plan1: I start with an analog sketch — outlining the structure of the problem, dependencies, and first ideas for the API. This helps me set priorities and break the work into small, testable steps.
sudoku.plan2: Then I move everything into Jira, where I create tasks and estimate them. This keeps the workflow organized and gives me a clear picture of what I’m doing and why.
sudoku.rowCheck1: I begin with the simplest element — a function that validates a Sudoku row. Following TDD principles:
sudoku.list_rowCheck1: I create a function rowCheck(row: number[]) that initially just returns false
sudoku.rowCheck2: I also considered testing non‑numeric values, but strong typing prevents such cases at compile time.
sudoku.rowCheck3: This is a great example of how types reduce the number of tests and speed up development.
sudoku.twoVersions1: During development I realize I need two variants:
sudoku.list_twoVersions1: rowCheck for the generator — it must require complete data (1–9).
sudoku.list_twoVersions2: rowCheck for the player — it must accept empty fields.
sudoku.twoVersions2: I add a separate Jira task to keep track of this and update the JSDocs to clearly describe the purpose of both functions.
sudoku.implementation1: I implement the logic: filtering elements and checking whether each appears exactly once.
sudoku.implementation2: A simple, clean function that is easy to test.
sudoku.architecture1: At this point I notice that I will need similar logic for:
sudoku.architecture2: Instead of duplicating code, I refactor the architecture:
sudoku.list4_architecture: separate data extractors for rows, columns, and sub‑squares.
sudoku.architecture3: This reduces complexity and makes testing easier.
sudoku.h_colExtractor: Column extractor tests and domain types
sudoku.colExtractor1: I start with a test: I expect the column extractor to return the correct array.
sudoku.colExtractor2: At this point I introduce domain‑specific types, such as Digit, to:
sudoku.list1_colExtractor: limit the range of allowed values,
sudoku.subsquares1: I write an initial draft of the sub‑square extractor — here I start with implementation, because it’s difficult to write a meaningful test without a minimal outline of the logic.
sudoku.subsquares2: Tests later stabilize the function and help refine the details.
sudoku.subsquaresTests1: I create tests that verify whether the extractor returns correct 9‑element arrays.
sudoku.subsquaresTests2: This ensures that the slicing logic works as intended.
sudoku.h_debugging: Debugging with the help of tests
sudoku.debugging1: A test that should pass unexpectedly fails.
sudoku.debugging2: I begin debugging — and here tests prove invaluable:
sudoku.list1_debugging: I discover the function returns void (I forgot the return statement),
sudoku.list2_debugging: after fixing that, the test still fails — the function returns a 3‑element array instead of a 9‑element one,
sudoku.list3_debugging: tests and console.logs lead me directly to incorrect slicing ranges.
sudoku.debugging3: This is a great example of how TDD + types let me focus on architecture and logic instead of remembering every detail.
sudoku.list2_summary: architectural thinking and refactoring during development,
sudoku.list4_summary: working with algorithms and structured data,
sudoku.summary2: These are not polished portfolio snippets — this is the actual process, visible in full context.
sudoku.summary3: At the beginning I was stressed by the recording and made a few silly mistakes (quickly corrected), but later the work went smoothly.
sudoku.summary4: The timelapse shows what a real, long development process looks like — not just curated highlights.

pat.title: Probably‑A‑Table — headless table library (npm)
pat.intro1: A custom headless UI library for React tables — modular, strongly typed, hook‑based, with pagination, sorting and drag‑and‑drop. It originated as a refactor of a nearly 600‑line prototype built in an MLOps project and was later published on npm.
pat.constext1: The project was created in an R&D setting.
pat.constext2: I initially built a quick, messy table prototype, partially generated by AI — I needed speed to validate an ML‑related idea. The table was only a small part of a larger system, so speed mattered more than code quality.
pat.constext3: When the prototype grew and became difficult to maintain, I decided to fully refactor it and create my own headless UI library that could be reused across multiple projects.
pat.constext4: (This is the opposite of the Sudoku case study, where I worked with TDD and clean architecture from the start. I adapt my workflow to the nature and experimental level of the project.)
pat.refactor1: The original table had nearly 600 lines of code.
pat.refactor2: Inside a single component, the following were mixed together:
pat.refactor3: It was completely non‑reusable and typical for R&D prototypes — fast, one‑off, with no architecture.
pat.list8_refactor: I split the component into small, specialized modules,
pat.list12_refactor: removed i18n from inside the table to avoid forcing any translation tool,
pat.list13_refactor: prepared space for optional translation overrides in future versions.
pat.refactor5: Result: around 80 lines of code, 18 of which are imports — clean, modular architecture.
pat.recaftor_before: Everything tangled together — classic for R&D prototypes that usually end up in the trash. For code meant to live longer than 3 days, this is obviously unacceptable.
pat.recaftor_after: Full separation of concerns, clean test‑covered code, domain‑driven architecture, strong generic typing and flexible API. The library is now lightweight, modular, extensible and easy to integrate into any project.
pat.h_hooks: Untangling stateful logic and creating custom hooks
pat.hooks1: In the prototype, all table logic — state, sorting, pagination, filters, drag‑and‑drop, and even i18n — lived inside one huge component. I wanted to start the refactor by extracting pure functions, but quickly discovered that almost everything depended on state.
pat.hooks2: So the first step was moving logic into custom hooks to:
pat.hooks3: I also removed i18n from inside the table — in the prototype it conflicted with the host project's i18n. I didn’t want to force any specific tool.
pat.hooks4: The library has internal translations, and future versions will include optional API for overriding them.
pat.hooks5: Below is a fragment of one of the hooks — responsible for sorting:
pat.hooks6: In the prototype, sorting was implemented inline, with logic, UI and state mixed together.
pat.hooks7: After the refactor, I moved sorting into a dedicated useSort hook, which:
pat.list6_hooks: is fully generic (Data extends { id: string | number }),
pat.list7_hooks: has a clean, predictable API (sortedData, sort, toggleSort),
pat.list10_hooks: delegates logic to pure utility functions (sortColumn, toggleSortState),
pat.list13_hooks: is reusable in any project and with any UI.
pat.hooks8: Thanks to this, the table component became a thin presentation layer, and the sorting logic is:
pat.hooks9: I applied the same approach to pagination, filters, drag‑and‑drop and all other logic.
pat.h_components: Splitting into sub‑components and table API
pat.components1: Once the logic was moved into custom hooks, I could safely keep state outside UI components and start splitting the table into smaller parts.
pat.components2: This was the moment when the library began to take shape — the monolithic component turned into a composition of modules, each responsible for a clearly defined piece of functionality.
pat.list1_components: the UI became a thin presentation layer,
pat.list2_components: the logic was encapsulated inside hooks,
pat.list4_components: the library’s API became predictable and easy to extend.
pat.components4: The diagram above shows a simplified architecture of the library.
pat.components5: This structure is not accidental — it follows the principles of headless architecture:
pat.list5_components: TableFull is responsible for the full table structure,
pat.list6_components: TableHeader and TableBody are pure presentation components,
pat.list7_components: TableMenu, SimpleSearch and Filters use hooks and provide additional features,
pat.list8_components: the entire system is orchestrated by ProbablyATable, which acts as the composer and API integrator.
pat.components6: Thanks to this, the user of the library can:
pat.list10_components: or build their own table using only the hooks,
pat.list11_components: or override any part of the UI,
pat.list12_components: or replace the logic with their own.
pat.components7: This provides maximum flexibility while keeping the API consistent and predictable.
pat.types1: All types in the library are exported and based on generics.
pat.types2: This allows users to build their own components and logic on top of a solid, predictable foundation of domain types.
pat.h2_types1: Column type — the foundation of the entire library
pat.types4: This is the most important type in the library — it defines a single column and ties it directly to the Data type.
pat.list1_types: Extract<keyof Data, string> — Ensures that the column id always corresponds to an existing field in the data, while also requiring the key to be a string. This is crucial for:
pat.list2_types: render: (row: Data) => ReactNode — Gives full control over the UI. The table logic is typed and headless, but the appearance remains in the user’s hands.
pat.types6: This type demonstrates conscious API design, linking data with UI and using generics to ensure type safety.
pat.h2_types2: Filter type — generating API from the interface
pat.types7: This is the main type describing the filter API.
pat.types8: It contains an independent Filters generic, which allows the filter structure to be generated automatically from the user’s interface.
pat.list3_types1: full consistency between data and filters,
pat.types10: Starting from version 0.2, filters will become optional, and type names will be simplified.
pat.h2_types3: Main component props — consistent API based on mapped types
pat.types11: This is an example of using mapped types to automatically generate the filter structure based on the Filters interface.
pat.list5_types1: Data extends { id: string | number } — Ensures that each row has a unique identifier, which is essential for React, sorting and drag‑and‑drop.
pat.list6_types1: filters and defaultFilters — Generated automatically from Filters, eliminating the need to repeat type definitions manually.
pat.list7_types1: API consistency — This type ties together all parts of the library: columns, data, filters and pagination.
pat.types13: And at the same time remains simple and predictable for the user.
pat.types14: This is an example of designing an API that is:
pat.list12_types1: and uses TypeScript as a full type system, not just improved IntelliSense.
pat.tests1: I started writing tests only after the architecture was stable and the experiments were finished.
pat.tests2: This project was too experimental to begin with TDD — tests only made sense once I knew which parts were final and worth solidifying.
pat.tests3: After moving the logic into custom hooks, I was able to extract pure functions independent of state and UI. These are the functions I wrote unit tests for. Example:
pat.tests4: toggleSortState is a pure function responsible for updating the sorting state. It is:
pat.list4_tests: resistant to edge cases (same column, different column, null),
pat.tests5: I wrote unit tests for it in Vitest, covering all scenarios.
pat.tests6: The tests are fully typed, which further increases API safety.
pat.tests7: This fragment clearly shows how I break domain logic into small, pure, testable pieces — unlike the prototype, where sorting was mixed with UI and state.
pat.tests8: In addition to unit tests, I wrote an integration test that checks the interaction between two modules: toggleSortState and sortColumn.
pat.tests9: The test simulates the real sorting flow and verifies whether:
pat.list6_tests: changing the sorting state affects the actual order of the data,
pat.list7_tests: the sorting logic behaves consistently and predictably,
pat.list8_tests: the API behaves the same in isolation and in integration.
pat.tests10: Example: integration test for sorting logic
pat.tests11: This test checks the real data flow — from changing the sorting state to actually sorting the array.
pat.tests12: Thanks to this, I can be sure that the sorting logic is:
pat.tests13: I used RTL tests to verify the sanity of UI components and several key interactions.
pat.tests14: After migrating the library, some tests stopped working (even though they worked in the original MLOps project), so this issue is included in the roadmap.
pat.tests16: RTL tests verify the integration of UI with headless logic, including:
pat.list15_tests: passing columns to TableHeader and TableBody,
pat.list16_tests: triggering toggleSort when clicking a header,
pat.list18_tests: rendering the correct number of rows.
pat.tests17: The tests are fully typed and do not mock the entire table — they check real behavior of presentation components that delegate logic to hooks and utilities.
pat.h_lib: Extracting the table into a standalone library
pat.lib2: Once the architecture was stable and the logic separated from the UI, I moved the entire table code into a standalone library project.
pat.lib3: This allows me to develop it independently, use it across multiple projects, and maintain a consistent API.
pat.lib5: The library includes a built‑in demo that allows users to:
pat.list2_lib: test features without integrating with another project,
pat.list3_lib: iterate faster on API and UI.
pat.lib6: The demo serves both as documentation and as a testing environment.
pat.lib7: The library is bundled using tsup — a lightweight and fast bundler ideal for TypeScript libraries.
pat.lib8: Initially, before publishing to npm, I tested it outside the demo using yalc, which I had used previously in Angular projects.
pat.list5_lib: yalc has known issues with Vite,
pat.list6_lib: sometimes fails to refresh dependencies correctly,
pat.lib10: So I switched to the more stable npm link, which works reliably with Vite.
pat.lib11: The library is published on npm.
pat.lib13: The library already has several patches — I use it in other projects and fix anything that “itches”.
pat.lib14: I make sure not to introduce breaking changes.
pat.lib15: The only planned change is making filters optional — this will not break backward compatibility.
pat.skills1: This project demonstrates a wide range of competencies:
pat.list1_skills: Headless UI — full separation of logic from presentation,
pat.list7_skills: Refactoring a large component into a clean library,
pat.list8_skills: Iterative workflow: prototype → analysis → architecture → product.
pat.skills2: Probably‑A‑Table is an example of my full engineering process: fast prototype → analysis → refactor → architecture → library.
pat.list9_skills: the ability to perform fast R&D,
pat.list10_skills: and the ability to create clean, modular, reusable tools for other developers.
pat.skills4: It is a library born from a real need, developed through a full lifecycle, and now a stable, typed, headless tool ready to be used in any React project.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: Can I work well in a team?
faq.a1_1b: I’ve worked in teams many times, not only as a developer but also in leadership roles: from QA Lead, to Project Manager, to occasional Scrum Master, to running a publishing house and managing an editorial team and a product. I value transparency, psychological safety, high standards — combined with a human approach.
faq.a1_2: In non‑leadership roles I adapt to the team. If I see something that could be done differently, I communicate it as a suggestion — something that can be freely rejected, and I’m perfectly fine with that.
faq.a1_3: When needed, I can step into a leadership role. There were situations where I was asked to temporarily replace a manager during a longer absence, even while I was simultaneously a developer in a completely different team.
faq.a1_4: I understand my place in the team, and despite having a lot of leadership experience, I don’t interfere with management — I only propose options with reasoning when I see value.
faq.a1_5: I left the purely managerial path because I’m more interested in engineering (the biggest downside of being a manager? You don’t write code). Tech Lead in the future? Why not. I’m good at coordination, so as long as I don’t have to give up coding, the option stays open.
faq.a2_1: React lets me prototype quickly, and in my recent personal projects it’s the tool I reach for most often. Why not Angular? Because Angular requires a lot of setup and boilerplate, which takes time and can be frustrating. And I don’t like when something gets auto‑generated and later — when you need to change it — you don’t remember what was created where.
faq.a2_2: In React I can write a component straight from memory, and it’s wonderfully simple.
faq.a2_3: React also has fewer moving parts and fits my style well.
faq.a2_4: At the same time, React is popular on the market, which lets me specialise comfortably while still building my own projects in any accompanying technologies — without needing to specialise in anything beyond React.
faq.a2_6: I’m a fullstack developer in my personal projects and I generally have no problem writing backend code in Node.js — especially architecture, logic, algorithms, or the core mechanics. I don’t consider myself a deep backend expert — sometimes I need to refresh how to handle databases or setups — but it’s all doable because it’s still TypeScript. If a commercial project needs backend support, I can do it alongside frontend work. It’s always an interesting experience on large systems I can’t practice solo.
faq.a2_7: Commercially, however, I want to focus on React so I don’t spread myself too thin and can truly specialise in one well‑practiced path that brings real value to companies. I’m a generalist, but in commercial environments that simply doesn’t work.
faq.a2_8: In my own projects — especially R&D — it’s wonderful. That balance between private and commercial work is ideal for me.
faq.q3: Won’t I get bored doing frontend, considering I have R&D and game dev projects in my portfolio?
faq.a3_2: In commercial projects I implement someone else’s vision — and that’s a completely different mode of work than R&D. In game dev it’s easy to get emotionally attached to story, characters, mechanics — and then the friction hurts. Frontend is perfect in that regard: there’s nothing to get emotionally attached to. A form? A table? A list view? Even beautiful code architecture? These aren’t things that create emotional bonds.
faq.list_a3_1: Thanks to that, I can focus on pure engineering and:
faq.list_a3_2: I have no problem throwing away my solutions if someone doesn’t like them,
faq.a3_3: and I maintain a healthy distance from code and architecture.
faq.a3_4: Not getting emotionally attached to the product (which especially hurt in game dev) lets me write good code according to someone else’s vision — without a conflict between “mine” and “theirs”. I treat it as an intellectual challenge: to create code and architecture that meet any rational external requirements. And then even every fixed bug can be satisfying.
faq.q4: Will my personal projects ever conflict with commercial projects?
faq.a4_2: First: I don’t build personal products that could compete with the commercial projects I work on.
faq.a4_3: My personal projects are mostly R&D, tools, frameworks, experiments, odd modules, simulations, and other things no company is likely to ask me to build at work (and if they would, I’d decline to avoid conflict of interest). I’m not writing the next Facebook or a Jira clone.
faq.a4_4: I build things that exist only because my brain found them interesting.
faq.a4_5: Second: commercial frontend guarantees I won’t get emotionally attached, so there’s no risk of “getting in my own way” with personal projects.
faq.a4_6: What would I even get attached to? A form? A table? That’s the beauty of frontend: I can do great engineering without my brain switching into “this is my child, don’t touch it” mode.
faq.a4_7: And there’s a third element: it works like a perpetual motion machine.
faq.list_a4_1: Commercial projects deepen my React skills → I build better personal projects.
faq.list_a4_2: Personal projects deepen my R&D and tooling → I build better commercial frontend.
faq.a4_8: It’s the perfect balance: commercially I do stable engineering, and privately I can switch into “mad scientist mode” without limits. One fuels the other.

caseStudy.leadershipDescription: Projects where I led teams, structured workflows, coordinated cross‑disciplinary work and ensured delivery in environments with high responsibility, ambiguity or operational pressure. These experiences demonstrate my ability to guide teams, manage complexity, communicate clearly across roles and maintain both quality and momentum in demanding projects.
caseStudy.qaTitle: QA Leadership — Sony TRC Compliance & Team Management
caseStudy.qaDescription: As a QA Lead, I managed a team responsible for Sony TRC compliance and high‑risk testing tasks. I took on the most complex and responsibility‑heavy test cases myself, while coordinating, reviewing and supporting the work of the rest of the team. I built the operational backbone of the QA function: onboarding, knowledge base, best‑practice documentation, communication flows and reporting structures. I acted as the bridge between testers, developers and management — ensuring clarity, realistic timelines and stable delivery.
caseStudy.qaSkill_1: Managing and coordinating a QA team in a high‑responsibility environment
caseStudy.qaSkill_2: Taking ownership of the most complex and risk‑sensitive test cases
caseStudy.qaSkill_3: Designing onboarding processes, knowledge bases and best‑practice documentation
caseStudy.qaSkill_4: Reviewing and validating the work of testers, ensuring consistency and quality
caseStudy.qaSkill_5: Communicating issues, delays, risks and workload to developers and management
caseStudy.qaSkill_6: Maintaining alignment across QA, engineering and leadership
caseStudy.qaSkill_7: Ensuring compliance with Sony TRC requirements
caseStudy.qaSkill_8: Structuring workflows and expectations for junior testers
caseStudy.pmDescription: Project Manager for a highly complex, ambiguous and technically challenging initiative: migrating a long‑running web‑based MMO to mobile platforms. I coordinated a mixed‑seniority development team, managed priorities, structured tasks in Redmine (in the absence of Jira), and acted as the primary communication point between developers, company leadership and an international client. I handled estimations when developers struggled with scoping — breaking down tasks, clarifying requirements and producing realistic timelines. I navigated generational differences within the team (senior 50+ engineers and 20‑year‑old juniors), ensuring collaboration, clarity and psychological safety.
caseStudy.pmSkill_1: Leading a development team through a high‑ambiguity, multi‑layered migration project
caseStudy.pmSkill_2: Structuring tasks, priorities and workflows in Redmine
caseStudy.pmSkill_3: Acting as the communication bridge between developers, company leadership and external stakeholders
caseStudy.pmSkill_4: Producing realistic estimations and workload assessments based on technical understanding
caseStudy.pmSkill_5: Managing cross‑seniority team dynamics and resolving friction
caseStudy.pmSkill_6: Translating unclear or shifting requirements into actionable plans
caseStudy.pmSkill_7: Maintaining delivery momentum despite uncertainty and incomplete specifications
caseStudy.pmSkill_8: Coordinating a long‑term, multi‑stakeholder project without formal budget ownership
caseStudy.rodoCardTitle: GDPR Consent Architecture — end‑to‑end analysis & system design for telecom CRM/OSS/SEC
caseStudy.rodoCardDescription: A business‑analysis and system‑architecture project that began as a simple request to “design the GDPR consent checkboxes” — and evolved into a full forensic investigation of how a telecom CRM/OSS/SEC platform must process, store and expose personal data under multiple overlapping legal regimes. The work uncovered that historical invoices (pre‑GDPR) fall under different legal bases than post‑GDPR data, requiring distinct processing rules, retention logic and access‑control boundaries. I mapped how the CRM, BOK and backend layers would need to react to consent changes, pseudonymisation/anonimisation requirements, role‑based access constraints and audit‑logging obligations. The result was a complete cross‑layer design for a consent‑driven data‑processing architecture suitable for high‑volume telecom environments.
caseStudy.rodoSkill_1: Extracting real requirements from ambiguous or incomplete stakeholder requests
caseStudy.rodoSkill_2: Deep regulatory analysis (GDPR, pre‑GDPR invoice rules, legal bases for processing)
caseStudy.rodoSkill_3: Translating legal constraints into concrete system behaviour and architecture
caseStudy.rodoSkill_4: Designing consent‑driven workflows across CRM, BOK and backend services
caseStudy.rodoSkill_5: Modelling pseudonymisation/anonimisation logic and retention rules
caseStudy.rodoSkill_6: Defining role‑based access boundaries and audit‑logging requirements
caseStudy.rodoSkill_7: Mapping cross‑layer data flows and identifying systemic risks
caseStudy.rodoSkill_8: Documenting complex regulatory‑technical interactions in a clear, actionable form
caseStudy.rodoSkill_9: Aligning legal, business and engineering perspectives into a unified design
caseStudy.printCardTitle: Offset Printing Pipeline Integration — feasibility & requirements analysis for telecom CRM
caseStudy.printCardDescription: A business‑analysis and system‑design project evaluating the feasibility, scope and architectural impact of integrating an external offset‑printing pipeline with a large telecom CRM/OSS platform. The goal was to design a workflow where the CRM could automatically decide whether a document should be emailed or sent to print, based on GDPR consents, contract clauses and customer preferences — and then synchronise status updates with the printing house to keep the CRM and Customer Service systems aligned. The work required deep analysis of cross‑system data flows, regulatory constraints, legacy CRM logic and operational edge cases. I translated business intent into a clear architectural model, mapped dependencies across multiple teams and produced documentation that enabled the organisation to understand the real cost, risks and technical shape of the integration.
caseStudy.printSkill_2: Translating ambiguous business needs into clear, actionable requirements
caseStudy.printSkill_3: Regulatory analysis (GDPR‑driven decision logic, consent‑based workflows)
caseStudy.printSkill_4: Identifying feasibility constraints, risks and architectural implications
caseStudy.printSkill_5: Modelling multi‑step operational workflows (email vs print routing, status synchronisation)
caseStudy.printSkill_6: Documenting system behaviour, edge cases and integration boundaries
caseStudy.printSkill_7: Aligning business, legal and engineering perspectives in a single coherent model
caseStudy.crmCardDescription: A long‑lived, multi‑team telecom CRM/OSS/SEC platform built on a custom Groovy‑generated Angular SSR stack and deployed across twelve Docker containers. I worked on stabilising the system, analysing legacy behaviours and improving UX and operational reliability in an environment shaped by years of undocumented logic, inconsistent patterns and complex cross‑container interactions.
caseStudy.crmSkill_1: Established the first unified knowledge architecture (taxonomy, naming standards, rationale-based documentation)
caseStudy.crmSkill_2: Delivered research-informed UI stabilisation, legacy-system forensics and behavioural-pattern analysis across a 12-container Angular/Groovy SSR architecture.
caseStudy.behaviouralForensicsCardDescription: Delivered a neutral, mechanics‑based explanation that transformed an unresolvable dispute into an interpretable cognitive system, enabling clarity without blame.
caseStudy.behaviouralForensicsSkill_2: Reconstructed a workplace conflict using high‑resolution cognitive forensics.
caseStudy.behaviouralForensicsSkill_3: Validated high‑resolution cognitive modelling in a real‑world environment.
caseStudy.kitchenCardDescription: Performed full agent‑based simulations, modelling the messy kitchen as a cognitive‑environmental system, identifying friction points (sensory mismatch, object identity, protocol absence), and redesigning the environment to align with cognitive patterns.
caseStudy.kitchenSkill_1: Complete elimination of the “emergent life” cycle.
caseStudy.kitchenSkill_3: Demystified my frameworks for people seeing them for the first time.
caseStudy.cognitiveDescription: Projects where I applied cognitive modelling, behavioural forensics and system‑level analysis to understand, simulate and redesign complex human–environment interactions. These experiences demonstrate my ability to model cognitive states, analyse emergent behaviour, diagnose system‑level friction and translate abstract cognitive frameworks into practical, interpretable outcomes for real people and real environments.
caseStudy.misalignmentCardTitle: AI Behaviour Evaluation (FEDRI → LLM Misalignment Testing)
caseStudy.misalignmentCardDescription: Used FEDRI to generate agent‑based cognitive profiles under different stress and regulation states (CIR levels), then ran controlled conversations between these simulated agents and an LLM to evaluate misalignment, reasoning breakdowns, refusal patterns, and interpretive drift.
caseStudy.misalignmentSkill_1: Identified LLM blind spots across CIR‑based cognitive states.
caseStudy.misalignmentSkill_2: Reframed prompts that triggered edge‑case failures, restoring stability.
caseStudy.misalignmentSkill_3: Tested a wide range of dysregulated synthetic behaviours and analysed the model’s reactions.
caseStudy.misalignmentSkill_4: Mapped escalation, refusal, hallucination, and context‑loss patterns.
caseStudy.aiDescription: Projects where I explored practical machine‑learning integration, LLM behaviour, misalignment analysis and human–AI interaction. These experiences show my ability to work with ML models in real systems, design distributed architectures for experimentation, evaluate model behaviour under stress conditions and analyse failure modes in LLM reasoning.
caseStudy.publishingCardTitle: Publishing House Operations — process & team management
caseStudy.publishingCardDescription: Founder & operations lead for a small publishing house. Responsible for building and maintaining the operational backbone of the organization.
caseStudy.publishingSkill_1: Designed and managed end‑to‑end publishing workflows
caseStudy.publishingSkill_2: Coordinated work of editors, authors and contractors
caseStudy.publishingSkill_3: Built processes for quality control, scheduling and delivery
caseStudy.publishingSkill_4: Oversaw legal, organizational and compliance‑related aspects of publishing
caseStudy.publishingSkill_5: Structured internal documentation and operational standards
caseStudy.publishingSkill_6: Ensured smooth collaboration across creative and technical roles
caseStudy.cirSkill_1: Designed a psychoeducational system using proprietary emotional‑state modelling (CIR)
caseStudy.cirSkill_2: Built NLP‑based heuristics for early detection of psychological regression
caseStudy.cirSkill_3: Designed adaptive NPC behaviour driven by cognitive state changes
caseStudy.cirSkill_4: Completed investment readiness evaluation for 800,000 PLN (~$200,000)
caseStudy.cirSkill_5: Contributed to planning a projected 6M PLN (~$1.5M) development budget
caseStudy.cirSkill_6: Worked with institutional stakeholders and early‑stage regulatory considerations
caseStudy.cirCardTitle: CIR — Mental Health Med Tech Concept
caseStudy.cirCardDescription: A strategic R&D initiative combining cognitive science, med‑tech and early‑stage investment work. Included collaboration with KPT and preparation for large‑scale development.
caseStudy.strategicOperationsDescription: Projects where I operated beyond pure engineering or design — working at the intersection of strategy, organization, investment and operational processes. These experiences demonstrate my ability to collaborate with institutions, assess feasibility, structure complex initiatives, manage teams and build the operational foundations required for long‑term projects.
caseStudy.businessAnalysisDescription: Projects where I worked directly with stakeholders to understand business goals, uncover real needs, translate them into clear requirements and shape system architecture around them. These experiences show my ability to run structured discovery, work with legal and regulatory constraints (including GDPR), document complex systems and ensure that business intent is reflected in technical design.
caseStudy.architectureDescription: Projects where I designed systems from the ground up — from custom file formats and internal APIs to modular UI libraries and domain‑specific tooling. These experiences show my ability to think in terms of architecture, model complex domains, design scalable foundations and build systems that can evolve over time.
caseStudy.enterpriseDescription: Commercial projects delivered for large organizations, including telecom‑grade CRM systems and other enterprise‑level platforms. These experiences show my ability to work in environments with complex business logic, strict regulatory constraints, multi‑team coordination and long‑lived architectures. I’ve contributed to systems that require high reliability, structured processes, and deep understanding of domain‑driven workflows.
caseStudy.krystalikCardTitle: Krystalik - experimental writing tool with metadata engine (in development)
caseStudy.krystalikCardDescription: An experimental R&D project exploring how long‑form writing tools can integrate narrative metadata. Includes early prototypes of a text editor (MS Word–like) combined with systems for managing characters, timelines, locations and plot structures. Built on a custom, index‑based JSON file format designed for modular storage and fast retrieval of narrative components.
caseStudy.krystalikSkill_1: R&D‑driven system design (exploration of editor + metadata architecture)
caseStudy.krystalikSkill_2: Designing custom file formats (multi‑JSON, index‑based structure)
caseStudy.krystalikSkill_3: Data modeling for narrative entities (characters, timelines, scenes)
caseStudy.krystalikSkill_5: Internal API design for metadata operations
caseStudy.projectsDescription: Selected projects, each representing a different skill — from React architecture and async workflows, through algorithmic thinking, TDD, and solving complex problems, to large-scale production migrations and delivering end-to-end solutions. I chose them from many others so that together they form a complete picture of my experience: technical, architectural, product-oriented, and R&D-driven.
caseStudy.reactCoreDescription: Projects that showcase my main area of expertise: frontend architecture, library design, async workflows, and backend integration. This is where you can see how I think about APIs, DX, typing, testing, and component design. These are the most representative examples of my work as a React developer, and I can show the code.
caseStudy.gameDevDescription: Projects that highlight my creativity, adaptability, and ability to work within unusual constraints. They’re also part of my story — how I started programming and how I handled situations where I had to invent solutions because the tools simply didn’t offer any.
caseStudy.problemSolvingDescription: Projects that show how I approach difficult problems — from algorithms and TDD, through legacy work, to designing CSS systems and APIs. This is where my thinking process, iteration, engineering mindset, and ability to structure complexity are most visible.
caseStudy.end2endSolutionsDescription: Projects I delivered from idea to working product. They show that I can design systems, think about users, work within technical constraints, and bring a project all the way to publication. These are examples of real impact — both in education and in a commercial mobile game.
caseStudy.chaperoneCardTitle: Chaperone Directive — R&D in a legacy CRM
caseStudy.chaperoneCardDescription: An R&D-driven improvement to a 7-year-old legacy CRM: a system of “CSS chaperones” — a biochemistry-inspired logic layer that automatically organizes and enforces correct UI styling. Instead of rewriting the system, I built a mechanism based on Angular directives, DNA keys, and a single source of truth for styles. The solution neutralizes CSS chaos.
caseStudy.patCardTitle: Headless UI table library published on npm
caseStudy.patCardDescription: Probably-A-Table — my own headless UI table library for React, built on top of Radix and published on npm. Modular, fully typed, with custom hooks, pagination, sorting, drag-and-drop, and complete documentation.
caseStudy.sudokuCardDescription: I designed and implemented a Sudoku generator from scratch, using TDD. I recorded the entire process — from the first sketch on paper, through writing tests, to refactoring — as an 8-hour timelapse and turned it into a highlight series showing my real thinking and workflow. The project demonstrates how I approach algorithmic problems, design APIs, and iterate.
caseStudy.mlopsCardTitle: A full-stack sandbox for experimenting with ML models (in development)
caseStudy.mlopsCardDescription: A sandbox (emphasis on sandbox) for experimenting with ML models (e.g., GPT-2), built as a distributed architecture. The project demonstrates ML integration, a microservices setup, and modern React.
caseStudy.backendAsync: Handling async workflows in UI (polling, retry, fallback UI)
caseStudy.stateManaging: Advanced state management (Context, custom hooks, logic–UI separation)
caseStudy.backendIntegration: Integration with real backend (API Gateway, WebSockets, mocks)
caseStudy.originCardTitle: A project showing how I became a developer
caseStudy.originCardDescription: A story where I originally had a different role because I didn’t believe I was suited for programming — until I had to replace the developer and write the code myself.
caseStudy.crowCardTitle: When the game engine doesn’t allow two characters
caseStudy.crowCardDescription: Instead of modifying the engine, I wrote an abstraction layer on top of it, using the dialogue bubble animation system to animate a second character.
caseStudy.makoCardDescription: I used my own cognitive frameworks and game dev knowledge to improve learning experiences in a school environment, which directly affected grades and attendance. I designed and built a system that supported this and received an mBank “mPotęga” grant.
caseStudy.rndFrameworks: Original cognitive & R&D frameworks (SPINE, CIR, FEDRI, MAKO)
caseStudy.highImpact: Significant improvement in student motivation, grades, and attendance
caseStudy.visixCardTitle: Optimization & maintenance of a warehouse app (React)
caseStudy.reactCommercialDescription: Commercial projects where I worked on large, long-lived React systems. These experiences show my ability to work in complex codebases, optimize performance, debug difficult issues, and lead technological migrations in active products.
caseStudy.visixCardDescription: A warehouse management application developed over many years by multiple teams. I worked on improving performance, stability, and code quality — from eliminating unnecessary rerenders to fixing bugs that the maintenance team couldn’t resolve.
caseStudy.debug: Debugging difficult issues in large production codebases
caseStudy.legacyReact: Working with legacy React written by many developers
caseStudy.tasks: Implementing new features based on business requirements
caseStudy.labirynthCardDescription: Labyrinth of Motivation — a logic game with a non-invasive ad SDK (hints could be purchased by watching ads), published on Google Play (now unavailable).
caseStudy.multiskill: Code, music, animation, graphics, gameplay design
caseStudy.publishingProcess: Publishing as a Google Play developer
caseStudy.comfortelCardTitle: Migration of a large Customer Service system from React JS (class) to React TS (functional)
caseStudy.comfortelCardDescription: A full migration of a large Customer Service system from class components to functional components, along with introducing TypeScript. I was responsible for typing from scratch, architectural refactoring, and gradually phasing out legacy code.
caseStudy.migration: Migrating large systems from class to functional components
caseStudy.typeScript: Introducing TypeScript into an existing project
caseStudy.complexCode: Working in complex, long-lived production code with minimal regression risk

intro.newIntro_1: I design, analyse and stabilise complex systems — from enterprise‑grade CRM platforms and regulatory‑driven architectures to experimental R&D tools, cognitive frameworks and AI behaviour analysis.
intro.newIntro_2: My work sits at the intersection of engineering, architecture and R&D. I specialise in shaping complex ideas into clear, scalable systems — from early exploration to implementing solutions, combining deep analysis with practical, hands‑on delivery.
intro.list_newIntro_3_1: Architecture & system engineering — designing foundations, modelling domains, building tools, refactoring long‑lived systems.
intro.list_newIntro_3_2: Enterprise & telecom — stabilising multi‑container platforms, analysing legacy behaviours, shaping cross‑layer data flows.
intro.list_newIntro_3_3: Business analysis — extracting real needs from stakeholders, translating regulations into system behaviour, documenting complex processes.
intro.list_newIntro_3_4: Compliance & legal‑technical design — GDPR, consent architectures, retention logic, pseudonymisation/anonimisation, platform rules.
intro.list_newIntro_3_5: R&D & cognitive systems — modelling behaviour, analysing emergent dynamics, building experimental frameworks and simulations.
intro.list_newIntro_3_6: AI & ML — distributed sandboxes, LLM misalignment testing, human–AI interaction, behavioural forensics.
intro.list_newIntro_3_7: End‑to‑end delivery — from idea to working product, including mobile, web, game dev and educational systems.
intro.newIntro_4: Across all these areas, my strength is system thinking: understanding how parts interact, where friction emerges, how behaviour propagates through architecture, and how to redesign systems so they become stable, predictable and maintainable.
intro.newIntro_5: I work equally well in greenfield R&D, legacy forensics, regulatory environments and high‑stakes enterprise systems — always with the same goal: bring clarity, structure and long‑term stability to complex problems.
intro.me: Who I Am as an Engineer
intro.about1: I'm a React Developer with a strong background in architecture and R&D.
intro.about2: I combine classic frontend engineering with a research‑driven approach: I like breaking systems down into parts, modelling dependencies, designing modules, and building solutions that not only work but also make sense as components of a larger whole.
intro.about3: In commercial work I focus on stable, predictable engineering — React, TypeScript, UI architecture, state, async flows, integrations. In my personal projects I switch into R&D mode: I build tools, frameworks, simulation modules and various experimental constructs that exist simply because my brain found them interesting.
intro.about4: This balance works like a perpetual motion machine: commercially I deepen my React skills → I build better personal projects, and privately I deepen my R&D → I build better commercial frontend.
intro.about5: I like architecture because it gives me space for system‑level thinking.
intro.about6: I like React because it lets me iterate quickly.
intro.about7: I like R&D because it lets me ask questions that don’t have ready‑made answers.
intro.in_1: If you're curious how I design architecture and refactor along the way, check out my npm library — it's a good example of how I approach modularity and iteration.
intro.in_2: If you want to see how I write code in real time, take a look at the Sudoku project — it includes a full recording of the process.
intro.in_3: And if you want to see more, feel free to explore the Projects section. You'll find the full spectrum there: from stable engineering and commercial work to full “mad scientist mode” in end‑to‑end R&D.
intro.in_4: If you have questions or doubts — the answer may already be in the FAQ. I like putting there things people tend to ask sooner or later (including things I’d probably ask myself).
intro.in_5: And for the brave ones who want to see my brain from the inside — you're welcome to visit my fully self‑ironic blog.

sandbox.title: Sandbox Playground
sandbox.info: Here you can drop your UI experiments, animations, Radix, Framer Motion work, layout tests, components, prototypes, and so on.
